5 min read
Just now

--

What interests me here is not simply that NRED may have two technology platforms. It is that MetalCore and EyeX are aimed at two different parts of the same exploration decision.
MetalCore helps answer where the company should focus. EyeX could help show what happens while the work is being carried out. One platform organizes geological intelligence. The other could add operational visibility. Together, they point toward a more connected exploration model.
MetalCore Helps Decide Where to Work
Exploration companies rarely begin with no information. More often, the useful evidence is scattered across historical reports, regional surveys, geological maps, sample databases and previous field programs. MetalCore is designed to bring that information together and turn it into clearer exploration priorities.
In July 2026, NRED reported that MetalCore had compiled public information relating to the Wilmac Copper-Gold Project, including 10 mineral occurrences, approximately 19 assessment reports filed between 1968 and 2025, 38 regional geochemical samples, historical production records and regional aeromagnetic data. The platform used those inputs to organize a prioritized target set that can be refined through modern sampling, geophysics and drilling.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is the first layer of intelligence. MetalCore does not replace field geology. It helps NRED decide where fieldwork may create the most useful new information, making exploration more focused before crews, equipment and capital are committed.
EyeX Could Show What Happens Next
Once a target becomes part of a field program, the questions change. Did the crew reach the planned area? Is equipment moving on schedule? Has someone entered a restricted zone? Is there smoke, unusual vehicle activity or a possible fuel leak? Are access, communications or weather affecting the work?
These are operational questions rather than geological ones. EyeX describes a computer-vision platform that can connect cameras, drones, vehicles and edge devices, identify relevant events and deliver alerts to the appropriate responder. Its proposed mining applications include equipment monitoring, perimeter control, smoke and fuel-leak detection, personnel awareness and remote-site alerts.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In July 2026, NRED entered a 30-day standstill agreement with EyeX while the two sides conduct mutual due diligence regarding a potential transaction involving an interest in the technology. The company also appointed EyeX co-founder Valentin Saitarli as chief technology officer, with a mandate that includes integrating technology initiatives into exploration operations.
That appointment may become an important bridge between NRED’s digital tools and its physical exploration work. MetalCore can help shape the plan. An EyeX-type layer could help NRED observe how that plan is executed.
The Middle of Exploration Is Usually Hard to See
Exploration results are normally presented as a sequence of outputs: samples collected, surveys completed, targets refined and drilling planned. What is less visible is everything that happens between the plan and the result.
Crews travel across difficult terrain. Equipment moves between work areas. Weather changes the schedule. Access delays one target while another receives more attention. Safety or environmental events can interrupt a program. Operational intelligence could create a clearer record of that middle stage.
Imagine MetalCore identifies a high-priority area at Wilmac. The field team travels to the location, completes sampling or geophysics and returns new evidence to the geological model. An operational layer could add context around that work: when the crew reached the area, how long equipment was active, whether access caused delays and whether any event required a response.
The complete sequence becomes:
target priority → field activity → physical result → updated decision
MetalCore provides the geological reasoning. EyeX could add visibility around execution. The value comes from connecting the two.
Wilmac Gives NRED a Practical Environment
Wilmac could provide the physical setting where this broader technology model develops. NRED’s planned 2026 program includes expanded soil sampling and four IP/AMT survey grids across North Lamont, West Lamont, Wilmac and Plume. The company has also outlined an initial drilling program as the project advances.
MetalCore can help organize the historical and regional evidence behind those work areas. Field teams can test the priorities through sampling, geophysics and eventually drilling. An operational-intelligence layer could help document equipment activity, site access and relevant events during that work.
This would give NRED more than a record of what the ground revealed. It could also provide a clearer picture of how efficiently each target was tested. That distinction matters because exploration outcomes are shaped by both geology and execution.
Better Visibility Can Improve the Next Program
Suppose two target areas produce different results. The difference may be geological, but it may also reflect uneven sampling, delayed access, incomplete coverage or equipment downtime. A stronger operational record helps separate those factors.
It can also improve planning. If one type of terrain repeatedly slows field teams, future programs can allocate more time or different equipment. If particular camera or drone positions provide better visibility, they can be reused. If alerts improve response around restricted zones or fuel storage, the same workflow can expand to other locations.
In this model, operational intelligence makes geological intelligence more actionable. The field program does not simply produce data for MetalCore. It also produces information about how the work itself can improve.
A More Connected Exploration Model
Traditional exploration creates value by acquiring promising ground, collecting evidence and testing geological ideas. NRED is developing a broader version of that model.
MetalCore gives the company a digital layer for understanding where to focus. EyeX could add a visual layer for understanding what happens while the work is underway. Wilmac provides a real exploration environment where those capabilities can begin to connect.
The result is not simply two separate technology products. It is a possible operating loop: geological data shapes the plan, field teams test it, operational intelligence adds context and the next decision becomes more informed.
Each part can be useful on its own. The larger strategic opportunity appears when information begins moving between them.
MetalCore helps NRED decide where to work. EyeX could help the company see how that work unfolds. Connecting those two questions could move NRED from target generation toward a more complete form of operational intelligence.
